That is because these master clips are actually saved in different folders, and are only being referenced by the sequence that is in the folder.
Enabling this setting allows you to see the referenced assets of a sequence, even if they do not exist in the same folder, when you look for it using the Browse app. This helps to avoid only seeing a sequence and then needing to duplicate the master clips used by adding it to the folder.
This setting is disabled by default, and is only available if an Avid Production Management system is deployed.
To enable the referenced assets in Browse app:
1. Click the User Profile button on the right side of the Fast Bar and select User Settings.
The User Settings dialog box opens.
2. In the navigation panel on the left, select the PM+ group.
3. Enable the toggle Show referenced assets in Browse app.
4. Click Save.
The following illustration shows a folder where a sequence is saved, but with the referenced assets setting disabled. Only the sequence that has been saved is displayed inside the folder.
A User Settings The next illustration shows the same folder where the sequence is saved, but this time with the referenced assets setting enabled. As the sequence is composed of two master clips saved in different folders, you will be able to see those as well. The master clips are greyed out to indicate that they are referenced assets.
B Keyboard Shortcuts B Keyboard Shortcuts This topic provides information about keyboard shortcuts:
- "General Shortcuts" below
- "Asset Editor Shortcuts" on the next page
> **Note:** These keyboard shortcuts can be used on Windows systems or macOS systems, unless otherwise
noted. In some cases the macOS uses the Command key in place of the Ctrl key.
> **Note:** Keyboard shortcuts are designed for an American keyboard and might not work with other keyboard
layouts.
General Shortcuts These shortcuts apply to multiple areas of the user interface. The following shortcuts might be app-specific and might not apply to all apps or menus.
Shortcut Description Focus Enter or Return Opens drop down menu when header is on focus — such as the Multiple areas Display Options menu in the Browse app Enter or Return Selects in-focus value or performs action of an in-focus button Multiple areas Up arrow Navigate up through a menu Multiple areas Down arrow Navigate down through a menu Multiple areas Tab Navigates to the next field or area of the user interface. Multiple areas Shift+Tab Navigates to the previous field or area of the user interface. Multiple areas Esc Escapes / closes out of a context menu or drop-down menu without Multiple areas applying changes Closes Filters fly-out windows, such as those in the Search app.
Esc Closes the User Settings window User Settings Esc Closes the Notifications window Notifications Ctrl+C Copy something to the clipboard Multiple areas B Keyboard Shortcuts Shortcut Description Focus Ctrl+V Paste something from the clipboard Multiple areas Ctrl+click (Win) Open multiple instances of the same app Fast Bar See "Opening Multiple App Instances" on page 47.

Media Viewer (Source mode) A Move the position indicator to the previous segment in the Media Viewer Timeline. The position indicator moves to the first frame after (Record mode) the cut. In a News Sequence, the position indicator moves to the closest segment on an active track.
S Move the position indicator to the next segment in the Media Viewer Timeline. The position indicator moves to the first frame after (Record mode) the cut. In a News Sequence, the position indicator moves to the closest segment on an active track.
If you move the position indicator to the end of a sequence, the position indicator moves to the final frame of the sequence.

Shortcut Description Focus Ctrl+S Save story General Ctrl+E Toggle the story’s edit lock on and off General Shift+Left Arrow Select text one character at a time moving backward Story section Shift+Right Arrow Select text one character at a time moving forward Story section Shift+Up Arrow Select text one line at a time moving upward Story body Shift+Down Arrow Select text one line at a time moving downward Story body Ctrl+] Split the segment Story body Ctrl+X Cut selection Story section Ctrl+C Copy selection Story section Ctrl+V Paste selection Story section Ctrl+B Set selected text to bold Cue List/Story body Ctrl+I Italicize selected text Cue List/Story body Ctrl+U Underline selected text Cue List/Story body Ctrl+Alt+P Turn on Presenter Instruction mode or mark selected text as Story body presenter instructions Alt+Insert Insert production cue Story body Shift+Alt+Insert Insert primary cue Story body Ctrl+Alt+M Mark selected text as machine control instructions Cue List Ctrl+Alt+C Turn on Closed Captioning mode or mark selected text as closed Story body captioning text Ctrl+Alt+N Turn on Normal text mode or change selected text to normal font Cue List/Story body Ctrl+Alt+G Create a Story Group Queue section Ctrl+Alt+V Dissolve a Story Group Queue section Tab Move to next editable field. For more information, see "Tab Rundown app Navigation" below.
Shift+Tab Move to previous editable field. For more information, see "Tab Rundown app Navigation" below.
Ctrl+Shift+I Insert a script template Story body Shift+Ctrl+F Toggle display of the story form Rundown app Ctrl+P Open the Queue/Story Print dialog Rundown app B Keyboard Shortcuts Tab Navigation Navigation, using the Tab key, within the Rundown app varies depending on where focus is located within the app. Generally, pressing Tab will navigate "forward" to the next editable field, and pressing Shift+Tab will navigate "backward" to the previous editable field.
For example, if in the Queue panel, pressing Tab navigates to the next editable field in the selected
- row. Otherwise, it goes to the first editable field in the story form, if open, or then to the first segment
in the story body.
- For example, if in the Story Form panel, pressing Tab navigates to the next editable field if any;
otherwise, it goes to the first story segment.
For example, if in the Story body, pressing Tab navigates to the next segment if any.

- While working in a pill, the tab key moves the cursor
to the next available field.
- If you are on the final field, the tab key creates a new
pill with the input field in focus.
